title: Exceptions
subtitle: Catch problems before they crash your program — and keep running with a friendly message.
goal: Understand what exceptions are, catch the common ones with try/catch, use multiple catch and finally, read the exception hierarchy, throw your own exceptions, and write a custom exception class.

blocks.heading: Errors are normal 🛡️
blocks.html:
Even the best programs hit problems: a number divided by zero, a missing piece of data, text that was supposed to be a number but wasn't. In Java these problems are called exceptions. They are not a sign you are a bad coder — they happen to everyone, every day.

The trick is to expect them. Instead of letting an exception crash your whole program, you can catch it and print a friendly message, then carry on. That's what today is all about: programs that bend instead of break.

blocks.heading: What a crash looks like
blocks.html:
When an exception is not handled, Java stops your program and prints a scary-looking stack trace. You may have seen these names before:

  • ArithmeticException — dividing a whole number by zero.
  • ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException — reaching for an array slot that doesn't exist.
  • NullPointerException — using something that is null (empty/missing).
  • NumberFormatException — turning text like "banana" into a number.

Today we'll stop these from crashing anything.

blocks.heading: try and catch: your safety net
blocks.html:
You wrap the risky code in a try block. If something goes wrong inside, Java jumps to the matching catch block instead of crashing.

try { risky code } catch (ExceptionType e) { what to do if it breaks }

The e is the exception object — it carries details, like a message describing what went wrong.

blocks.caption: Catching an out-of-bounds reach
blocks.output:
First score: 90
Now reaching for score number 5...
Oops! That spot in the array does not exist.
The program keeps going. No crash!
blocks.explain: The array only has spots 0, 1, and 2. Reaching for scores[5] throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, so Java skips the rest of the try and jumps to catch. The 'This line never runs' message is skipped, but the program finishes cleanly.

blocks.html: Only wrap the risky part. Don't put your whole program in one giant try block. Wrap just the lines that might fail, so you know exactly what you're protecting.

blocks.caption: Catching divide-by-zero and bad text
blocks.output:
100 / 10 = 10
Cannot divide 100 by 0 — skipping!
100 / 5 = 20
'banana' is not a number, so I will use 0 instead.
All done, safe and sound.
blocks.explain: Dividing 100 by 0 throws ArithmeticException — we catch it and skip that one, then keep looping. Integer.parseInt("banana") throws NumberFormatException because 'banana' isn't a number. Both are caught, so the program runs to the friendly final line.

blocks.heading: Many catches, and a finally that always runs
blocks.html:
One try can have several catch blocks, each handling a different kind of problem. Java runs the first one that matches.

You can also add a finally block. The code in finally runs no matter what — whether the try succeeded or an exception was caught. It's perfect for clean-up steps you always want to happen.

blocks.caption: Multiple catch blocks plus finally
blocks.output:
Trying word: 7
  Length 1, number is 7
  Finished checking this word.
Trying word: oops
  That word is not a number!
  Finished checking this word.
Trying word: null
  That word was missing (null)!
  Finished checking this word.
Every word handled. Nothing crashed.
blocks.explain: "7" works fine. "oops" has a length but isn't a number, so NumberFormatException is caught. null can't even do .length(), so NullPointerException is caught. Notice the finally line prints EVERY time — success or failure — because finally always runs.

blocks.heading: The exception family tree 🌳
blocks.html:
All exceptions belong to one big family. At the top is Throwable. Below it sits Exception, and below that is RuntimeException:

Throwable → Exception → RuntimeException

The four common ones you met today (ArithmeticException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, NullPointerException, NumberFormatException) are all RuntimeExceptions. Because they share a family, you could even catch them all with a single catch (Exception e) — though catching the specific type is usually clearer.

Checked vs unchecked (light version): RuntimeExceptions are unchecked — Java doesn't force you to handle them. Other Exceptions (like the custom one we make next) are checked — Java makes you either catch them or mark the method with throws. Don't worry about memorizing this; just know that's why custom exceptions sometimes need a throws word.

blocks.heading: Throwing your own exceptions
blocks.html:
You don't have to wait for Java to throw an exception — you can throw one yourself with throw new SomethingException("message"). This is how you say "this situation is not allowed!"

You can even make your own exception type by extending Exception. A custom exception is just a small class with a clear name, like TooYoungException.

blocks.caption: A custom exception class (Main + one helper class in the same file)
blocks.output:
Age 15 is allowed in. Welcome!
Sorry: Age 9 is too young — must be 13 or older.
Door check complete.
blocks.explain: TooYoungException is our own class — it extends Exception and passes a message to super(). checkAge throws it when the age is under 13. Because it's a checked exception, checkAge is marked 'throws TooYoungException', and main catches it. e.getMessage() reads back the message we created. The public class Main comes first; the non-public TooYoungException sits below it in the same file.

blocks.html: Same-file rule: a file can hold ONE public class (here, Main), plus any number of non-public classes (like TooYoungException). Put Main first so java Main.java knows where to start.

blocks.question: What does a try/catch let your program do when something goes wrong?
options: Catch the problem and keep running instead of crashing
blocks.explain: try runs the risky code; if it throws an exception, catch handles it with a friendly message and the program keeps going — no crash.

blocks.question: When does the code inside a finally block run?
options: Only when an exception is caught
Always — whether or not an exception happened
blocks.explain: finally always runs: after a successful try, and also after a caught exception. That's why it's great for clean-up steps.

blocks.question: Which line correctly throws a custom exception with a message?
blocks.explain: You raise an exception with throw new SomethingException("message"). The message is passed into the exception so catch can read it with getMessage().

blocks.title: Survive divide-by-zero
blocks.instructions: The loop divides 100 by each number, but one of them is 0. Fill in the catch so an ArithmeticException is handled with a friendly message instead of crashing. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — dividing by zero no longer crashes your loop!
blocks.failMsg: The exception thrown by 100 / 0 is an ArithmeticException — put that type inside the catch parentheses.

blocks.title: Handle bad text
blocks.instructions: Integer.parseInt("five") will throw a NumberFormatException because "five" is not digits. Fill in the catch type so it prints a friendly message. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Great — your program politely handles text that isn't a number!
blocks.failMsg: Turning "five" into an int throws a NumberFormatException — use that type in the catch.

challenge.title: Build a custom exception
challenge.instructions: Write a custom exception called NotEnoughMoneyException (extends Exception). In buyTicket(int money), if money is less than 10, throw new NotEnoughMoneyException with a message saying how many coins there are; otherwise print that a ticket was bought. main already loops over {15, 4} and catches the exception. Keep public class Main FIRST and the exception class below it. Press Check.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Ticket bought with 15 coins!
No ticket: Need 10 coins, but only have 4.
Ticket booth closed.
challenge.hint: Make a class NotEnoughMoneyException extends Exception with a constructor that calls super(message). Inside buyTicket, use 'if (money < 10) throw new NotEnoughMoneyException(...)'. Remember: public class Main goes first, the exception class goes below it.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You designed your own exception and threw it on purpose — that's how real programs guard against bad situations. Day 16 complete!
challenge.failMsg: Make sure you have a 'class NotEnoughMoneyException extends Exception' with a super(message) constructor, and that buyTicket throws it when money < 10.
